comment_text.sn11.11:1.2: The oldest stories of Indra already evidence a shift from the violent victory over the serpent Vṛtra (see SN 2.3:3.4) to his liberating the Vala cave, which he did by the power of truth in his role as bṛhaspa﻿ti, the “lord of hymns”. Curiously, just as this discourse follows one featuring Sambara, later legend connects Indra’s power in this life to advice he received from Sambara in the past; namely, to always honor and respect brahmins (Mahābhārata 13.36).
comment_text.sn11.11:1.3: The seven vows and following verses also appear in the next two discourses.
comment_text.sn11.11:2.1: Here jantu shows its original meaning of “one who is born”, namely a human child.

root_text.sn11.11:1.2: “Sakkassa, bhikkhave, devānamindassa pubbe manussabhūtassa satta vatapadāni samattāni samādinnāni ahesuṁ, yesaṁ samādinnattā sakko sakkattaṁ ajjhagā.
root_text.sn11.11:1.8: yāvajīvaṁ vigatamalamaccherena cetasā agāraṁ ajjhāvaseyyaṁ muttacāgo payatapāṇi vossaggarato yācayogo dānasaṁvibhāgarato,
root_text.sn11.11:1.10: yāvajīvaṁ akkodhano assaṁ—sacepi me kodho uppajjeyya, khippameva naṁ paṭivineyyanti.
root_text.sn11.11:1.11: Sakkassa, bhikkhave, devānamindassa pubbe manussabhūtassa imāni satta vatapadāni samattāni samādinnāni ahesuṁ, yesaṁ samādinnattā sakko sakkattaṁ ajjhagāti.

translation_text.sn11.11:1.2: “Mendicants, in a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he undertook seven vows. And it was because of undertaking these that he achieved the status of Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.4: As long as I live, may I support my parents.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.5: As long as I live, may I honor the elders in the family.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.6: As long as I live, may I speak gently.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.7: As long as I live, may I not speak divisively.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.8: As long as I live, may I live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.9: As long as I live, may I speak the truth.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.10: As long as I live, may I be free of anger, or should anger arise, may I quickly get rid of it.
translation_text.sn11.11:1.11: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he undertook seven vows. And it was because of undertaking these that he achieved the status of Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.11:2.1: A child who respects their parents,
translation_text.sn11.11:2.2: and honors the elders in the family,
translation_text.sn11.11:2.3: whose speech is gentle and courteous,
translation_text.sn11.11:3.1: who’s committed to getting rid of stinginess,
translation_text.sn11.11:3.2: is truthful, and has mastered anger:
translation_text.sn11.11:3.3: the gods of the thirty-three declare
translation_text.sn11.11:3.4: them to be a true person.”

variant_text.sn11.11:0.3: Vatapadasutta → devā (sattavanapada) suttaṁ (bj); devasuttaṁ (1) (cck); paṭhamadevasuttaṁ (sya1ed, sya2ed); devā or vatapada (1) (pts1ed)
